design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate side-scrolling and top-down perspectives while eliminating enemy waves, collecting weapon power-ups, and reaching stage bosses. Weapon swapping and upgrade mechanics drive tactical decision-making.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource management of limited lives/continues against escalating enemy density and boss patterns; choice of which weapon upgrade path to pursue
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Elite commando (Bill or Lance) fighting through alien-infested military base and hostile terrain with escalating firepower and tactical versatility
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Hybrid perspective design (side-scrolling + top-down overhead stages) distinguishes it from original Contra; weapon upgrade system (dual power-up collection) and hyper shell mechanic add strategic depth; two-player independent continues system encourages cooperative play without forced synchronization

difficulty.lives_system: Limited lives per credit; players begin with starting credit and can use up to 4-6 additional credits depending on DIP switch settings
difficulty.continues_system: Each player can continue independently at any time without waiting for partner to exhaust lives (unlike original Contra). Two-player games result in fewer total continues available to clear game.
difficulty.checkpointing: Stage-based checkpoint system; no mid-stage saves. Defeated mid-bosses remain defeated within a stage.
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: DIP switch settings control credit limits (4 vs 6). Japanese version features arcade loop mode with highest difficulty forced on second loop and continues disabled—extreme skill gate for mastery

progression.upgrade_system: Weapon power-up system: four weapon types (machine gun, spread gun, bomb gun, laser gun) obtained from flying capsule drops. Weapons can be upgraded once by collecting same type twice in succession for increased destructive power. Hyper shell item (stage-clearing bomb) can be collected multiple times and carries over between overhead stages.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Stage select available in Japanese version (Hold Down+Select at title screen) allowing non-linear stage access for skilled players. Accessibility feature suggests progression gating is intentional.
replayability_hooks: Arcade loop mode (Japanese arcade version): second playthrough at highest difficulty with no continues
Two-player cooperation with asymmetric character colors (Bill=green, Lance=purple)
Stage select cheat for alternative routing
Hidden sound test (Hold A+B, press Start)
30-lives cheat code (regional variation: 30 lives JP/EU, 10 lives US)
Multiple weapon upgrade paths creating different run profiles

world_building_techniques: Stage geography progression: military base exterior → jungle → alien lair (environmental storytelling through location escalation)
Enemy type evolution: corrupted allied troops (possessed soldiers) then mutated alien creatures

Regional censorship differences: EU version replaces human protagonists with robots (RD008, RC011) and changes enemy sprites to robots due to German violence laws—implicit commentary on theme adaptation

boss_design.philosophy: Pattern-based encounters anchoring each stage's climax. Bosses are specialized threats distinct from regular enemy types, requiring weapon selection and positioning strategy.
notable_bosses: Level 1 Boss: Mid-stage and final encounter at military base
Level 5 Boss: Possesses unused flash-red mechanic (indicates damage threshold design intent)
Level 6 Boss (Serpent/Worm): Has 4 pre-programmed path options; only 2 are used in final design (suggesting iterative refinement or technical constraints)
First Boss (EU version): Graphically modified from JP/US version due to German content laws

faction_indicators: Bill Rizer vs Lance Bean: asymmetric color coding (green vs purple) for player identification in multiplayer—enables spectator faction alignment
Human protagonists (JP/US) vs Robot protagonists (EU): territorial/cultural identity markers through localization
danger_signals: Alien-possessed allied troops: betrayal/infiltration motif signals internal military compromise
Escalating enemy density waves: mounting pressure creates tension narrative independent of story exposition
Boss health thresholds (unused flash-red mechanic): mechanical communication of vulnerable states creates danger momentum
environmental_storytelling: Military base structural progression (exterior → interior): player penetrates deeper into compromised facility
Jungle stage: contrast between ordered base and chaotic natural terrain suggests alien terrain adaptation
Alien lair (exterior → interior): climactic environment signal through architectural scale and alien aesthetic
Overhead perspective stages: shift to top-down view during base/lair interiors creates disorientation/vulnerability effect

sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - Super C NES article)
wikipedia (Super Contra article with NES port details)
